stem: A 32-year-old woman attends her 6-week postnatal check. She was diagnosed with gestational diabetes at 26 weeks gestation, managed with metformin and diet. Her baby was born at 39 weeks weighing 3.8 kg with no neonatal complications. She is breastfeeding well and has stopped metformin since delivery. Her fasting BSL this morning was 5.2 mmol/L.

data_table.Investigations: Fasting BSL 5.2 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step regarding her glucose metabolism?

options.text: Reassure and advise annual fasting BSL
options.rationale: Annual fasting BSL alone is insufficient follow-up. Women with GDM require a formal 75 g OGTT at 6-12 weeks postpartum to exclude persistent glucose intolerance, as fasting glucose alone has poor sensitivity for IGT. (Source: ADIPS GDM Guidelines 2024)

options.text: Restart metformin and monitor BSL diary
options.rationale: Restarting metformin is premature. The fasting BSL of 5.2 is not diagnostic of diabetes, and a formal OGTT is needed first to classify her postpartum glucose status before initiating therapy. (Source: ADIPS GDM Guidelines 2024)

options.text: Arrange a 75 g oral glucose tolerance test
options.rationale: A 75 g OGTT at 6-12 weeks postpartum is recommended for all women with GDM. This is the appropriate time point and test to reclassify glucose status, as up to 30% of women with GDM will have IGT or overt T2DM on testing. (Source: ADIPS GDM Guidelines 2024; RANZCOG GDM Statement)

options.text: Check HbA1c and review in three months
options.rationale: HbA1c at 6 weeks postpartum is unreliable due to the physiological red cell turnover changes of pregnancy and blood loss at delivery. The OGTT is the recommended postpartum test. (Source: ADIPS GDM Guidelines 2024)

options.text: Defer glucose testing until 12 months postpartum
options.rationale: Deferring to 12 months risks missing early T2DM or IGT. ADIPS recommends testing at 6-12 weeks postpartum, with the 6-week postnatal visit being a practical opportunity. Ongoing annual screening follows thereafter. (Source: ADIPS GDM Guidelines 2024)

explanation.summary: Women with GDM have a 7-fold increased lifetime risk of developing T2DM. ADIPS recommends a 75 g OGTT at 6-12 weeks postpartum to reclassify glucose tolerance. Fasting glucose and HbA1c have insufficient sensitivity to detect IGT in this setting. If normal, annual screening with fasting BSL or HbA1c is then appropriate, with repeat OGTT if high risk.
key_points: 75 g OGTT is the recommended postpartum test for GDM, not fasting glucose or HbA1c alone
Timing is 6-12 weeks postpartum
Up to 30% of women with GDM have persistent glucose abnormality postpartum
Annual screening is recommended lifelong after GDM
explanation.pearls: HbA1c is unreliable in the early postpartum period due to peripartum blood loss and increased red cell turnover - the OGTT at 6-12 weeks remains the standard post-GDM reclassification test per ADIPS.

stem: A 38-year-old primigravida presents at 11 weeks gestation for her first antenatal visit. Her BMI is 34 kg/m2. She has chronic hypertension managed with labetalol 200 mg twice daily. Her blood pressure today is 128/82 mmHg. She has no proteinuria. Her mother had pre-eclampsia in her first pregnancy.

data_table.Investigations: Urine dipstick: protein negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate prophylactic intervention at this visit?

options.text: Commence aspirin 150 mg nightly from now
options.rationale: This woman has multiple risk factors for pre-eclampsia: age over 35, primigravity, BMI over 30, chronic hypertension, and family history. RANZCOG and SOMANZ recommend low-dose aspirin (100-150 mg) commenced before 16 weeks, ideally from 12 weeks, taken at night. (Source: RANZCOG Pre-eclampsia Guideline; SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Commence aspirin 100 mg daily from 16 weeks
options.rationale: While 100 mg is an acceptable dose, starting at 16 weeks is suboptimal. The benefit of aspirin for pre-eclampsia prevention is greatest when commenced before 16 weeks gestation. This patient is already 11 weeks, so starting now maximises benefit. (Source: RANZCOG Pre-eclampsia Guideline)

options.text: Commence calcium 1.5 g daily supplementation
options.rationale: Calcium supplementation (1-1.5 g daily) may reduce pre-eclampsia risk in women with low dietary calcium intake, but it is not the primary evidence-based prophylaxis. Aspirin has stronger evidence and should be commenced first. Calcium is an adjunct, not a substitute. (Source: WHO Calcium Supplementation Guideline; RANZCOG)

options.text: Increase labetalol to achieve BP below 120/80
options.rationale: Tight blood pressure control does not prevent pre-eclampsia, which is a placental-mediated disease. The current BP of 128/82 is acceptable for chronic hypertension in pregnancy. Over-aggressive lowering may compromise uteroplacental perfusion. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Monitor with serial uterine artery Dopplers
options.rationale: Uterine artery Doppler assessment at 20-24 weeks may help stratify risk, but it does not replace aspirin prophylaxis. Aspirin should be commenced based on clinical risk factors without waiting for Doppler results. (Source: RANZCOG Pre-eclampsia Guideline)

explanation.summary: Low-dose aspirin (100-150 mg at night) commenced before 16 weeks gestation reduces pre-eclampsia risk by approximately 60% in high-risk women. Risk factors include chronic hypertension, primigravity, maternal age over 35, BMI over 30, and family history. The ASPRE trial demonstrated that 150 mg aspirin from 11-14 weeks is most effective. RANZCOG recommends screening at the first antenatal visit.
key_points: Aspirin 100-150 mg commenced before 16 weeks gestation for pre-eclampsia prevention
Nocturnal dosing is recommended based on circadian blood pressure patterns
Multiple moderate risk factors or any single high-risk factor triggers prophylaxis
Aspirin is continued until 36 weeks gestation
explanation.pearls: Aspirin 150 mg nightly commenced at 11-14 weeks reduces preterm pre-eclampsia by 62% (ASPRE trial) - the benefit diminishes substantially when commenced after 16 weeks.

stem: A 34-year-old woman at 36 weeks gestation presents for her scheduled ultrasound review. She had a low-lying placenta identified at her 20-week morphology scan. Today's transvaginal ultrasound shows the placental edge is 18 mm from the internal cervical os. She has had no vaginal bleeding during the pregnancy. Her previous two births were uncomplicated vaginal deliveries.

data_table.Investigations: TVS: placental edge 18 mm from internal os, cephalic presentation, EFW 2850 g

lead_in: What is the most appropriate delivery plan?

options.text: Elective caesarean section at 37 weeks gestation
options.rationale: Elective caesarean is recommended when the placental edge is less than 20 mm from the internal os. At 18 mm, this is borderline and would typically meet criteria. However, RANZCOG guidance now supports a trial of labour when the edge is 11-20 mm in multiparous women with no bleeding history, making this not the most appropriate plan. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia Statement)

options.text: Elective caesarean section at 39 weeks gestation
options.rationale: While aiming for later gestation is desirable for neonatal outcomes, delaying to 39 weeks with a low-lying placenta (18 mm from os) carries increased risk of antepartum haemorrhage. If caesarean is planned, 37-38 weeks is the typical window for placenta praevia. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia Statement)

options.text: Repeat ultrasound at 38 weeks before deciding
options.rationale: Further delay in planning is not recommended. At 36 weeks, the placental position is unlikely to change significantly, and a definitive plan should be made. Continuing to defer decision-making increases the risk of emergency presentation. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Antepartum Haemorrhage)

options.text: Plan a trial of vaginal delivery with monitoring
options.rationale: With the placental edge 11-20 mm from the internal os in a multiparous woman with no bleeding history, a cautious trial of labour is reasonable per RANZCOG. Key conditions include delivery in a facility with immediate caesarean capability, senior obstetric presence, and cross-matched blood available. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia Statement; RCOG Green-top Guideline 27a)

options.text: Perform amniocentesis for foetal lung maturity
options.rationale: Amniocentesis for lung maturity is rarely performed in modern obstetric practice and is not indicated here. The gestation is already 36 weeks, and delivery timing should be based on placental position and clinical factors, not lung maturity testing. (Source: RANZCOG Placenta Praevia Statement)

explanation.summary: Placenta praevia management depends on the distance from the placental edge to the internal cervical os. When the edge is more than 20 mm away, vaginal delivery is generally safe. When it covers the os (true praevia), caesarean is mandatory. The 11-20 mm zone is managed individually, with trial of labour considered in multiparous women without prior bleeding. All deliveries should occur in facilities equipped for emergency caesarean.
key_points: Placental edge >20 mm from os: vaginal delivery appropriate
Placental edge <10 mm or covering os: caesarean delivery
Edge 11-20 mm: individualised decision based on parity and bleeding history
Delivery planning should be finalised by 36 weeks
explanation.pearls: The 11-20 mm zone from the internal os is the decision-making grey area for low-lying placenta - multiparity and absence of prior APH favour trial of labour, while nulliparity or any bleeding history favours caesarean at 37-38 weeks.

subtopic_detail: IOL prostaglandin choice in scarred uterus
stem: A 30-year-old woman at 41+3 weeks gestation is being planned for induction of labour. She had one previous lower segment caesarean section for breech presentation. She desires a vaginal birth after caesarean. Her cervix is 1 cm dilated, 50% effaced, firm, posterior, with the presenting part at station -2. The foetal cardiotocograph is reassuring.

data_table.Investigations: CTG: reassuring, Bishop score 4, cephalic presentation, EFW 3.6 kg

lead_in: What is the most appropriate method for cervical ripening?

options.text: Vaginal prostaglandin E2 dinoprostone gel insertion
options.rationale: Prostaglandin E2 preparations (dinoprostone gel or pessary) are associated with a 2-3 fold increased risk of uterine rupture in women with a prior caesarean scar compared to mechanical methods. They are generally avoided for VBAC induction in Australian practice. (Source: RANZCOG VBAC Statement; Queensland Clinical Guideline: IOL)

options.text: Transcervical Foley catheter balloon for ripening
options.rationale: A transcervical Foley catheter (mechanical ripening) is the preferred method for cervical ripening in women with a uterine scar attempting VBAC. It carries the lowest risk of uterine rupture compared to prostaglandins, while effectively achieving cervical ripening. (Source: RANZCOG VBAC Statement; Queensland Clinical Guideline: IOL)

options.text: Oral misoprostol 25 mcg administered two-hourly
options.rationale: Misoprostol (prostaglandin E1) is contraindicated for induction in women with a prior caesarean scar due to the highest reported risk of uterine rupture among all induction agents. This applies to both oral and vaginal routes. (Source: RANZCOG VBAC Statement; AMH)

options.text: Dinoprostone controlled-release vaginal pessary
options.rationale: The controlled-release dinoprostone pessary (Cervidil/Propess) still carries the prostaglandin-associated risk of uterine rupture in scarred uteri. While removal is possible, it is not recommended as first-line for VBAC induction when mechanical options are available. (Source: RANZCOG VBAC Statement)

options.text: Commence oxytocin infusion without prior ripening
options.rationale: Oxytocin may be used cautiously in VBAC, but with an unfavourable cervix (Bishop score 4), commencing oxytocin without prior cervical ripening has low success rates. Mechanical ripening first is more appropriate when the cervix is not favourable. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guideline: IOL)

explanation.summary: Induction of labour in women with a previous caesarean scar requires careful method selection to minimise uterine rupture risk (baseline ~0.5% for spontaneous VBAC, rising to 1-2% with prostaglandins). Mechanical methods such as the transcervical Foley catheter are preferred as they achieve cervical ripening without increasing rupture risk. Prostaglandins - both PGE2 (dinoprostone) and PGE1 (misoprostol) - are associated with significantly higher rupture rates and are avoided.
key_points: Foley catheter is preferred for cervical ripening in VBAC
Misoprostol is contraindicated with prior caesarean scar
PGE2 (dinoprostone) also carries increased rupture risk
Oxytocin can be used cautiously after cervical ripening
explanation.pearls: Uterine rupture risk in VBAC: 0.5% with spontaneous labour, 0.8% with oxytocin alone, 1-2.5% with dinoprostone, and up to 6% with misoprostol - making mechanical ripening the preferred induction method for scarred uteri.

stem: A 29-year-old woman has a normal vaginal delivery at 39 weeks. During the third stage of labour, she receives oxytocin 10 IU intramuscularly for active management. Twenty minutes after placental delivery, estimated blood loss reaches 700 mL with ongoing bleeding. The uterus feels boggy on palpation. Bimanual uterine compression is commenced, and 40 IU oxytocin in 1 L normal saline is running as an infusion. She has a history of well-controlled asthma.

data_table.Examination: Uterus boggy, placenta complete on inspection, no vaginal lacerations

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: Administer sublingual misoprostol 800 mcg
options.rationale: Misoprostol is an effective second-line agent but is generally reserved when other uterotonics have failed or are contraindicated. Ergometrine is preferred as the immediate second-line agent in women without hypertension, as it has faster onset and more predictable effect. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guideline: PPH)

options.text: Give intramuscular carboprost 250 mcg
options.rationale: Carboprost (Hemabate, PGF2-alpha) is contraindicated in asthma as it causes bronchospasm. This patient has a history of asthma, making carboprost unsafe. (Source: AMH; Queensland Clinical Guideline: PPH)

options.text: Administer tranexamic acid 1 g IV over 10 min
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid should be given as an adjunct in PPH, but it is an antifibrinolytic and not a uterotonic. The immediate priority with uterine atony is to achieve uterine contraction with a second-line uterotonic agent. TXA should be administered but does not replace the need for uterotonics. (Source: WOMAN Trial; Queensland Clinical Guideline: PPH)

options.text: Administer IV oxytocin 5 IU slow bolus only
options.rationale: Additional oxytocin boluses beyond what has been given (IM bolus plus IV infusion) risk receptor saturation and cardiovascular side effects (hypotension, tachycardia). The oxytocin receptor is already being maximally stimulated; escalation to a different class of uterotonic is needed. (Source: AMH; RANZCOG PPH Guideline)

options.text: Give intramuscular ergometrine 250 mcg
options.rationale: Ergometrine (or ergometrine-oxytocin combination) is the recommended second-line uterotonic when oxytocin fails and there is no contraindication (hypertension, cardiac disease). It produces sustained uterine contraction. The asthma history is not a contraindication to ergometrine. (Source: RANZCOG PPH Guideline; Queensland Clinical Guideline: PPH)

explanation.summary: The Australian PPH uterotonic escalation ladder begins with oxytocin (IM bolus and IV infusion), then ergometrine if no hypertension contraindication, then carboprost (if no asthma), then misoprostol. Tranexamic acid 1 g IV should be given concurrently within 3 hours of onset but is not a uterotonic. In this asthmatic patient, carboprost is contraindicated, making ergometrine the appropriate second-line choice.
key_points: Ergometrine is second-line after oxytocin in normotensive patients
Carboprost (PGF2-alpha) is contraindicated in asthma
Ergometrine is contraindicated in hypertension and cardiac disease
TXA is an adjunct antifibrinolytic, not a uterotonic replacement
explanation.pearls: Carboprost (PGF2-alpha) causes bronchospasm and is contraindicated in asthma; ergometrine causes vasoconstriction and is contraindicated in hypertension - knowing these contraindication pairs drives the uterotonic escalation pathway in PPH.

stem: A 27-year-old woman presents at 7 weeks gestation with mild left iliac fossa pain. She is haemodynamically stable with no peritoneal signs. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an empty uterus and a 22 mm left adnexal mass with a yolk sac but no foetal cardiac activity. There is a small amount of free fluid in the pouch of Douglas. Her serum beta-hCG is 3200 IU/L.

data_table.Investigations: Beta-hCG 3200 IU/L, TVS: empty uterus, 22 mm left adnexal mass with yolk sac, no FCA, trace free fluid

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Expectant management with serial beta-hCG
options.rationale: Expectant management is only appropriate when beta-hCG is less than 1500 IU/L and declining, the ectopic is small (less than 20 mm), and there is no visible cardiac activity. This patient's beta-hCG of 3200 and mass of 22 mm exceed the criteria for safe expectant management. (Source: RANZCOG Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Repeat beta-hCG in 48 hours before deciding
options.rationale: The diagnosis of ectopic pregnancy is already established on ultrasound (adnexal mass with yolk sac, empty uterus). Further beta-hCG monitoring delays definitive management and risks tubal rupture. Immediate treatment is needed. (Source: RANZCOG Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Administer single-dose methotrexate 50 mg/m2 IM
options.rationale: While methotrexate is a medical option for ectopic pregnancy, the beta-hCG of 3200 IU/L exceeds the typical Australian threshold for safe medical management (most centres use less than 3000-5000 IU/L depending on protocol). More importantly, the presence of a yolk sac indicates a more advanced ectopic with lower likelihood of methotrexate success. (Source: RANZCOG Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline; RCOG Green-top Guideline 21)

options.text: Proceed with laparoscopic salpingectomy
options.rationale: Laparoscopic surgery is the most appropriate management. The beta-hCG of 3200, adnexal mass of 22 mm with a yolk sac, and free fluid collectively indicate an advancing ectopic that is less likely to respond to methotrexate. Salpingectomy is preferred over salpingotomy unless the contralateral tube is absent or damaged. (Source: RANZCOG Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline; RCOG Green-top Guideline 21)

options.text: Perform diagnostic laparoscopy to confirm first
options.rationale: The ectopic is already confirmed on ultrasound - a visible adnexal mass with yolk sac and empty uterus is diagnostic. Diagnostic laparoscopy alone without planned treatment is an unnecessary step; the surgery should be therapeutic from the outset. (Source: RANZCOG Ectopic Pregnancy Guideline)

explanation.summary: Ectopic pregnancy management depends on haemodynamic stability, beta-hCG level, ultrasound findings, and patient factors. Methotrexate is appropriate for small unruptured ectopic pregnancies with beta-hCG generally below 3000-5000, no cardiac activity, and patient able to attend follow-up. Larger ectopics, higher beta-hCG, presence of yolk sac or cardiac activity, and significant free fluid favour surgical management. Salpingectomy is preferred unless there are fertility concerns about the contralateral tube.
key_points: Surgical management preferred when beta-hCG >3000-5000 or ectopic >35 mm or yolk sac/cardiac activity present
Salpingectomy preferred over salpingotomy when contralateral tube is normal
Methotrexate requires reliable follow-up and no contraindications (hepatic/renal/haematological)
Expectant management only for beta-hCG <1500 and declining with small ectopic
explanation.pearls: The presence of a yolk sac within an ectopic pregnancy mass approximately halves the success rate of methotrexate compared to an undifferentiated adnexal mass - this finding shifts the balance toward surgical management.

stem: A 31-year-old woman at 9 weeks gestation presents with vaginal spotting. Transvaginal ultrasound shows a gestational sac measuring 28 mm with no foetal pole, consistent with an anembryonic pregnancy. She is haemodynamically stable with minimal bleeding. She has no allergies and no significant medical history. She expresses a preference to avoid surgery if possible.

data_table.Investigations: TVS: 28 mm gestational sac, no foetal pole, no yolk sac, Hb 128 g/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Offer misoprostol 800 mcg vaginally with review
options.rationale: Medical management with misoprostol 800 mcg (vaginal or sublingual) is appropriate for confirmed first-trimester missed miscarriage when the patient prefers to avoid surgery. Success rates are approximately 80-85% within 7 days. She should receive written information about expected bleeding and when to present to emergency. (Source: RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss Guideline; Queensland Clinical Guideline: Early Pregnancy Loss)
source_refs: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Early Pregnancy Loss

options.text: Recommend expectant management for two weeks
options.rationale: Expectant management has lower success rates (approximately 50-60% at 2 weeks for anembryonic pregnancy) compared to medical management. While it is a valid option, it would not be the most appropriate initial approach given her preference to avoid surgery - medical management offers a better success rate while still avoiding surgical intervention. (Source: RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss Guideline)

options.text: Arrange immediate surgical evacuation of uterus
options.rationale: Surgical evacuation is effective and appropriate, but the patient has expressed a preference to avoid surgery. Medical management should be offered first as it aligns with her wishes and has good efficacy. Surgery remains available if medical management fails. (Source: RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss Guideline)

options.text: Administer mifepristone 200 mg orally and review
options.rationale: Mifepristone alone without misoprostol is not the standard protocol. The combination of mifepristone 200 mg followed by misoprostol 800 mcg 24-48 hours later is used in some protocols and improves success rates, but mifepristone alone is insufficient. The standard single-agent approach is misoprostol. (Source: RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss Guideline)

options.text: Repeat ultrasound in one week to confirm diagnosis
options.rationale: The diagnosis of anembryonic pregnancy is confirmed - a gestational sac of 28 mm with no foetal pole meets the diagnostic criteria (sac greater than or equal to 25 mm with no embryo). Repeating the ultrasound delays management without clinical benefit. (Source: RANZCOG Early Pregnancy Loss Guideline)

explanation.summary: First-trimester miscarriage can be managed expectantly, medically, or surgically. Medical management with misoprostol achieves complete passage in 80-85% of cases. The diagnosis of anembryonic pregnancy is confirmed when a gestational sac reaches 25 mm or more without an embryo. Patient preference should guide management choice when all options are clinically reasonable. Surgical evacuation is reserved for patient preference, failed medical/expectant management, or heavy bleeding.
key_points: Anembryonic pregnancy diagnosed when gestational sac >=25 mm without embryo
Misoprostol 800 mcg vaginally is first-line medical management
Success rate of medical management approximately 80-85%
All patients need safety-netting advice about heavy bleeding and infection signs
explanation.pearls: Adding mifepristone 200 mg 24-48 hours before misoprostol improves complete miscarriage rates from approximately 80% to 90% in missed miscarriage - though single-agent misoprostol remains the standard first-line protocol in many Australian centres.

sources.label: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Early Pregnancy Loss

stem: A 24-year-old woman presents with a 3-year history of worsening dysmenorrhoea and deep dyspareunia. Her pain is cyclical, beginning 2 days before menses and lasting 5 days. She has tried paracetamol and ibuprofen with limited relief. She is not planning pregnancy. Clinical examination reveals tenderness in the posterior fornix with nodularity of the uterosacral ligaments. Pelvic ultrasound shows a 25 mm left ovarian endometrioma.

data_table.Examination: Posterior fornix tenderness, uterosacral nodularity bilaterally
data_table.Investigations: Pelvic USS: 25 mm left ovarian endometrioma, normal right ovary, anteverted uterus

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial pharmacological management?

options.text: Commence naproxen 500 mg twice daily perimenstrually
options.rationale: NSAIDs provide symptomatic relief but do not suppress the underlying endometriosis or prevent disease progression. This patient has already tried ibuprofen with limited benefit. Hormonal therapy aimed at suppressing oestrogen-dependent endometrial implant activity is the first-line approach. (Source: RANZCOG Endometriosis Guideline)

options.text: Refer for diagnostic and therapeutic laparoscopy
options.rationale: Laparoscopy is not required before commencing empirical hormonal therapy when the clinical and ultrasound findings are consistent with endometriosis. Surgery can be considered if hormonal therapy fails or if tissue diagnosis is needed for treatment planning. (Source: RANZCOG Endometriosis Guideline; ESHRE Endometriosis Guideline)

options.text: Commence combined oral contraceptive pill continuously
options.rationale: Continuous combined oral contraceptive pill (COCP) is a first-line hormonal treatment for endometriosis in women not planning pregnancy. It suppresses ovulation and reduces menstrual flow, thereby reducing oestrogen-driven endometrial implant activity. Continuous use (skipping the pill-free interval) is preferred to cyclic use to eliminate withdrawal bleeds. (Source: RANZCOG Endometriosis Guideline; Therapeutic Guidelines)

options.text: Start depot leuprorelin 3.75 mg monthly injection
options.rationale: GnRH agonists (leuprorelin) are effective but are second-line due to significant side effects including menopausal symptoms and bone density loss. They require add-back HRT if used beyond 6 months. Simpler first-line hormonal options should be tried initially. (Source: RANZCOG Endometriosis Guideline; AMH)

options.text: Commence oral medroxyprogesterone acetate 30 mg daily
options.rationale: High-dose progestogens are effective for endometriosis but carry more side effects (weight gain, mood changes, breakthrough bleeding) than the COCP. They are generally reserved for women who cannot take oestrogen-containing preparations or as second-line therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines; AMH)

explanation.summary: Endometriosis affects approximately 10% of reproductive-age women and is the most common cause of secondary dysmenorrhoea. First-line hormonal treatment includes the COCP used continuously or a levonorgestrel IUD. GnRH agonists and high-dose progestogens are second-line. Surgery is considered when medical management fails or for fertility-related concerns. A tissue diagnosis via laparoscopy is no longer required before commencing empirical hormonal therapy when clinical and imaging findings are consistent.
key_points: COCP continuously is first-line hormonal therapy for endometriosis
Laparoscopy is not required before starting empirical hormonal treatment
GnRH agonists are second-line due to bone density effects
Continuous use of COCP is preferred over cyclical use
explanation.pearls: Continuous COCP use (no pill-free interval) reduces endometriosis pain more effectively than cyclical use - the 7-day hormone-free interval allows reactivation of endometrial implants and return of symptoms.

stem: A 26-year-old woman with a BMI of 32 presents with oligomenorrhoea, acne, and hirsutism. She has been diagnosed with polycystic ovary syndrome based on Rotterdam criteria. She is not planning pregnancy currently. Her fasting glucose is 5.4 mmol/L and her 75 g OGTT 2-hour result is 8.8 mmol/L. Total cholesterol is 5.6 mmol/L with LDL 3.4 mmol/L. She has tried lifestyle modification for 6 months with a 2 kg weight loss.

data_table.Investigations: Fasting glucose 5.4 mmol/L, 2-hour OGTT 8.8 mmol/L, HbA1c 38 mmol/mol, Total cholesterol 5.6 mmol/L, LDL 3.4 mmol/L, HDL 1.1 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate additional pharmacological intervention for her metabolic risk?

options.text: Commence atorvastatin 20 mg nightly for lipids
options.rationale: While her lipids are borderline elevated, her absolute cardiovascular risk is low given her age. Statin therapy is not first-line for a 26-year-old. Addressing insulin resistance is more important at this stage, as it drives both the metabolic and reproductive features of PCOS. (Source: RACGP Red Book; Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular)

options.text: Add pioglitazone 15 mg daily as insulin sensitiser
options.rationale: Pioglitazone is an insulin sensitiser but is not recommended for PCOS management due to weight gain, fluid retention, and fracture risk. Metformin is the preferred insulin-sensitising agent in PCOS. (Source: International Evidence-based Guideline for PCOS; AMH)

options.text: Add spironolactone 50 mg daily for hyperandrogenism
options.rationale: Spironolactone addresses the hyperandrogenic symptoms (hirsutism, acne) but does not address the metabolic risk. The question asks specifically about metabolic risk management, which the impaired glucose tolerance demands. Spironolactone may be added for cosmetic symptoms separately. (Source: International Evidence-based Guideline for PCOS)

options.text: Prescribe orlistat 120 mg with meals for weight loss
options.rationale: Orlistat may assist with weight loss but does not directly address insulin resistance. Given the impaired glucose tolerance (2-hour OGTT 8.8 mmol/L, above the 7.8 threshold), specific insulin-sensitising therapy with metformin is more appropriate. (Source: International Evidence-based Guideline for PCOS)

options.text: Commence metformin 500 mg and titrate to 1500 mg
options.rationale: Metformin is indicated in PCOS when impaired glucose tolerance (IGT) is present (2-hour OGTT 7.8-11.0 mmol/L). Her result of 8.8 mmol/L confirms IGT. Metformin improves insulin sensitivity, may restore menstrual regularity, and reduces progression to T2DM. Starting at 500 mg and titrating reduces GI side effects. (Source: International Evidence-based Guideline for PCOS; ADIPS)

explanation.summary: PCOS is the most common endocrine disorder in reproductive-age women and carries significant metabolic risks including insulin resistance, IGT, T2DM, and dyslipidaemia. The 2023 international evidence-based guideline recommends OGTT screening at diagnosis and periodically thereafter. Metformin is indicated when lifestyle modification is insufficient and IGT or metabolic features are present. It should be started at a low dose and titrated gradually to minimise gastrointestinal side effects.
key_points: OGTT screening recommended at PCOS diagnosis due to high IGT prevalence
Metformin indicated when IGT present despite lifestyle modification
IGT defined as 2-hour OGTT 7.8-11.0 mmol/L
Metformin also provides modest benefit for menstrual regularity and androgen levels
explanation.pearls: Women with PCOS and IGT have a 5-10 fold higher annual conversion rate to T2DM compared to women with IGT alone - metformin reduces this conversion rate by approximately 50% (Diabetes Prevention Program).

subtopic_detail: Cervical screening in pregnancy with oncogenic HPV
stem: A 29-year-old primigravida attends her first antenatal visit at 10 weeks gestation. She reports her last cervical screening test 18 months ago was HPV positive (not HPV 16/18) with a low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (LSIL) on liquid-based cytology. The recommendation was a repeat test at 12 months, which she has not yet attended. She is otherwise well with no history of abnormal bleeding.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate management regarding her cervical screening?

options.text: Defer all cervical screening until 6 weeks postpartum
options.rationale: Deferring entirely is not appropriate when a follow-up test was overdue. While cervical screening is generally deferred during pregnancy if the woman is up to date, this patient has an overdue follow-up for a prior abnormal result. Pregnancy does not preclude cervical screening. (Source: NCSP Clinical Guidelines; Cancer Council Australia)

options.text: Perform cervical screening test now at this visit
options.rationale: Cervical screening (HPV test with reflex cytology) can be safely performed during pregnancy and should not be deferred when a follow-up test is overdue. The prior result of non-16/18 HPV with LSIL required 12-month repeat, which is now overdue. The test is safe in pregnancy and the result will guide further management. (Source: NCSP Clinical Guidelines; Cancer Council Australia)

options.text: Refer directly for colposcopy without retesting
options.rationale: Colposcopy referral without retesting is not indicated. The original result was non-16/18 HPV with LSIL, which required 12-month repeat testing, not immediate colposcopy. Colposcopy is indicated if the repeat test shows persistent HPV or higher-grade abnormality. (Source: NCSP Clinical Guidelines)

options.text: Perform the screening test at second trimester visit
options.rationale: Waiting until the second trimester unnecessarily delays follow-up. The overdue test can be performed at any gestation. Earlier testing allows timely colposcopy referral if needed, as colposcopy is safe in pregnancy but cervical treatment is deferred until postpartum. (Source: NCSP Clinical Guidelines)

options.text: Arrange urgent colposcopy within two weeks
options.rationale: Urgent colposcopy is indicated for suspected invasive cancer or high-grade cytological abnormality, not for overdue follow-up of LSIL with non-16/18 HPV. The appropriate step is to repeat the screening test first to determine current HPV and cytology status. (Source: NCSP Clinical Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Cervical screening is safe during pregnancy and should be performed when overdue, particularly when following up prior abnormal results. Under the Australian NCSP renewed pathway, non-16/18 oncogenic HPV with LSIL requires repeat testing at 12 months. If the repeat shows persistent oncogenic HPV, colposcopy is indicated. Colposcopy can be safely performed during pregnancy, but excisional treatment is deferred until at least 6 weeks postpartum unless invasive cancer is suspected.
key_points: Cervical screening can be performed during pregnancy when overdue
Non-16/18 HPV with LSIL requires 12-month repeat, not immediate colposcopy
Colposcopy is safe in pregnancy but treatment is deferred postpartum
HPV 16/18 positive results trigger direct colposcopy referral regardless of cytology
explanation.pearls: Under the NCSP renewed pathway, HPV 16/18 positivity triggers direct colposcopy referral regardless of cytology result, while other oncogenic HPV types require reflex cytology to triage - this distinction drives the referral threshold.

stem: A 33-year-old woman who delivered vaginally 3 days ago wishes to discuss long-acting reversible contraception before discharge. She had an uncomplicated vaginal delivery at 38 weeks with a second-degree perineal tear repaired primarily. She is breastfeeding. She is interested in a copper intrauterine device as she prefers non-hormonal contraception. She has no history of postpartum haemorrhage or endometritis.

data_table.Examination: Uterus well contracted, lochia normal, perineal repair intact

lead_in: What is the most appropriate advice regarding copper IUD insertion timing?

options.text: Insert the IUD before discharge from hospital
options.rationale: While immediate post-placental IUD insertion (within 10-48 minutes of placental delivery) is safe and effective, insertion at 3 days postpartum falls in the higher-expulsion-risk window (48 hours to 4 weeks). The optimal approach at this point is to delay until 4-6 weeks postpartum. (Source: RANZCOG Contraception Guideline; FSRH UK MEC)

options.text: Plan insertion at the 6-week postnatal visit
options.rationale: IUD insertion at 4-6 weeks postpartum is the standard recommended timing if immediate post-placental insertion was not performed. By this time, uterine involution is well advanced, the risk of perforation is lower, and the expulsion rate is comparable to interval insertion. (Source: RANZCOG Contraception Guideline; FSRH Contraception Guideline)

options.text: Defer insertion until 12 weeks postpartum minimum
options.rationale: Waiting until 12 weeks is unnecessarily long and exposes the patient to unintended pregnancy risk. Uterine involution is adequately complete by 4-6 weeks for safe IUD insertion. The patient should be counselled about interim contraception if waiting. (Source: RANZCOG Contraception Guideline)

options.text: Insert the IUD at 2 weeks postpartum instead
options.rationale: Insertion between 48 hours and 4 weeks postpartum carries the highest risk of IUD expulsion and is generally avoided. The uterus is involuting and the cervix remains dilated, contributing to higher expulsion rates of up to 15-25%. (Source: FSRH Contraception Guideline; WHO MEC)

options.text: Recommend an IUD is unsuitable postpartum
options.rationale: IUDs are safe and effective postpartum - both copper and hormonal types. Breastfeeding is not a contraindication. The key consideration is timing of insertion. The copper IUD is an excellent choice for a patient wanting non-hormonal long-acting contraception. (Source: RANZCOG Contraception Guideline)

explanation.summary: IUD insertion postpartum follows specific timing windows. Immediate post-placental insertion (within 10-48 minutes) is safe and increasingly promoted. Between 48 hours and 4 weeks, expulsion rates are highest, so insertion is generally deferred. From 4-6 weeks onwards, insertion is safe with expulsion rates comparable to interval insertion. The copper IUD is compatible with breastfeeding and offers effective non-hormonal long-acting contraception.
key_points: Post-placental insertion (within 48 minutes) is safe and effective
48 hours to 4 weeks postpartum carries highest expulsion risk - avoid insertion
Standard postpartum insertion is at 4-6 weeks
Both copper and hormonal IUDs are compatible with breastfeeding
explanation.pearls: The 48-hour to 4-week postpartum window has IUD expulsion rates of 15-25%, compared to 3-5% for post-placental insertion and 2-5% for insertion at 6 weeks or later - making early postpartum counselling for immediate insertion critical.

subtopic_detail: Menopause local vs systemic HRT for GSM
stem: A 58-year-old woman presents with a 2-year history of vaginal dryness, dyspareunia, and recurrent urinary tract infections. She reached menopause naturally at age 52. She has no vasomotor symptoms. She tried vaginal moisturisers for 6 months with minimal improvement. She has a history of treated localised breast cancer 4 years ago, currently on tamoxifen with no evidence of recurrence. She is otherwise well.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate pharmacological treatment?

options.text: Commence systemic combined oestrogen-progestogen HRT
options.rationale: Systemic HRT is generally contraindicated in women with a history of oestrogen receptor-positive breast cancer. Even if ER status is unknown, systemic HRT poses an unacceptable risk of recurrence. Her symptoms are genitourinary, not vasomotor, so systemic therapy is also not the most targeted approach. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines; AMS Position Statement)

options.text: Commence systemic oestradiol patches at 50 mcg dose
options.rationale: Systemic oestrogen (even transdermal) is contraindicated in women with breast cancer history. Furthermore, her symptoms are limited to the genitourinary syndrome of menopause, which responds well to local therapy without systemic oestrogen exposure. (Source: AMS Position Statement; AMH)

options.text: Switch tamoxifen to an aromatase inhibitor instead
options.rationale: Switching to an aromatase inhibitor would worsen genitourinary symptoms as AIs further reduce circulating oestrogen. This does not address the GSM and may exacerbate vaginal atrophy. The oncology team manages tamoxifen/AI decisions based on cancer factors. (Source: AMS Position Statement)

options.text: Prescribe low-dose vaginal oestrogen cream topically
options.rationale: Low-dose vaginal oestrogen (cream, pessary, or ring) is the most appropriate treatment for genitourinary syndrome of menopause. Minimal systemic absorption occurs at recommended doses. It can be used in breast cancer survivors after discussion with the treating oncologist, as serum oestradiol levels remain within the postmenopausal range. (Source: AMS Position Statement; Therapeutic Guidelines)

options.text: Prescribe oral ospemifene 60 mg daily for symptoms
options.rationale: Ospemifene is a selective oestrogen receptor modulator approved for dyspareunia due to vulvovaginal atrophy, but its use in breast cancer survivors is not established as safe due to its oestrogenic effects on some tissues. Low-dose vaginal oestrogen has a better evidence base in this population. (Source: AMS Position Statement)

explanation.summary: Genitourinary syndrome of menopause (GSM) includes vaginal dryness, dyspareunia, and recurrent UTIs due to oestrogen deficiency in the urogenital tissues. Low-dose vaginal oestrogen is the gold-standard treatment, with minimal systemic absorption at recommended doses. In breast cancer survivors, it may be considered after discussion with the oncology team. Systemic HRT is contraindicated. Non-hormonal options include vaginal moisturisers and lubricants.
key_points: Low-dose vaginal oestrogen is first-line for GSM after non-hormonal measures fail
Systemic HRT is contraindicated in breast cancer survivors
Vaginal oestrogen at recommended doses produces minimal systemic absorption
Oncology team involvement is important when prescribing vaginal oestrogen to breast cancer survivors
explanation.pearls: Low-dose vaginal oestrogen (25 mcg oestradiol pessary or 0.5 g oestriol cream twice weekly) maintains serum oestradiol within the postmenopausal range (<20 pmol/L) - this minimal systemic absorption underpins its considered use in breast cancer survivors.

stem: A 25-year-old woman at 9 weeks gestation presents to the emergency department with a 3-week history of persistent nausea and vomiting. She is unable to tolerate any oral intake. She has lost 5 kg since her booking visit. She has had two previous presentations where she received IV fluids and was discharged. Urinalysis shows 3+ ketonuria. She appears dehydrated with dry mucous membranes.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 98/60 mmHg, HR 106 bpm, Temp 36.5, weight loss 5 kg (8% body weight)
data_table.Investigations: Urine ketones 3+, Na 134 mmol/L, K 3.1 mmol/L, Urea 8.2 mmol/L, Cr 82 micromol/L

lead_in: In addition to IV fluid resuscitation and antiemetics, what is the most important supplementation to commence?

options.text: Intravenous thiamine 100 mg before any dextrose
options.rationale: Thiamine (vitamin B1) must be administered before any dextrose-containing fluids in hyperemesis gravidarum. Prolonged vomiting depletes thiamine stores, and dextrose metabolism consumes remaining thiamine, potentially precipitating Wernicke encephalopathy. IV thiamine 100 mg should be given empirically. (Source: RCOG Green-top Guideline: Hyperemesis; Queensland Clinical Guideline: Hyperemesis)

options.text: Oral folic acid 5 mg daily supplementation
options.rationale: Folic acid supplementation is important in pregnancy but is not the priority intervention in acute hyperemesis with dehydration and ketonuria. She likely cannot tolerate oral supplements currently. Thiamine deficiency prevention is more urgent due to the risk of Wernicke encephalopathy. (Source: RCOG Green-top Guideline: Hyperemesis)

options.text: Intravenous potassium chloride 40 mmol infusion
options.rationale: Potassium replacement is important given her K of 3.1 mmol/L, but this is a standard electrolyte correction, not the most critical supplementation. Thiamine deficiency carries the risk of irreversible neurological damage (Wernicke encephalopathy) and must be prioritised, especially before any glucose administration. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Hyperemesis)

options.text: Oral pyridoxine 25 mg three times daily
options.rationale: Pyridoxine (vitamin B6) is a first-line antiemetic for mild nausea in pregnancy but is inadequate for severe hyperemesis gravidarum requiring hospital admission. Additionally, oral intake is not tolerated in this patient. Thiamine supplementation takes priority. (Source: RCOG Green-top Guideline: Hyperemesis)

options.text: Intramuscular vitamin B12 1000 mcg injection
options.rationale: Vitamin B12 deficiency is not an acute concern in hyperemesis gravidarum of 3 weeks duration. B12 stores last months to years. Thiamine (B1) is the vitamin of concern in prolonged vomiting due to its small body stores (only 2-3 weeks) and the risk of Wernicke encephalopathy. (Source: RCOG Green-top Guideline: Hyperemesis)

explanation.summary: Hyperemesis gravidarum with prolonged vomiting depletes thiamine (vitamin B1) stores within 2-3 weeks. Wernicke encephalopathy (confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia) is a rare but devastating complication. Thiamine must be given BEFORE any dextrose-containing IV fluids, as glucose metabolism depletes remaining thiamine. Standard management includes IV normal saline, electrolyte correction, antiemetics (ondansetron, metoclopramide), and thromboprophylaxis if immobile.
key_points: Thiamine 100 mg IV must precede any dextrose-containing fluids
Body thiamine stores deplete within 2-3 weeks of reduced intake
Wernicke encephalopathy is preventable but irreversible if established
Potassium and other electrolyte replacement is also important but secondary
explanation.pearls: Body thiamine stores last only 18-21 days - any patient with vomiting beyond 3 weeks should receive empirical IV thiamine before dextrose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy, which has a 20% mortality if untreated.

stem: A 28-year-old Rh D-negative woman at 14 weeks gestation presents after a fall resulting in direct abdominal trauma. She has no vaginal bleeding and the foetus appears well on ultrasound. Her indirect Coombs test from booking bloods at 10 weeks was negative. She has not received any anti-D immunoglobulin during this pregnancy. The father of the baby is Rh D-positive.

data_table.Investigations: USS: viable intrauterine pregnancy, no retroplacental haematoma, CTG not applicable at this gestation

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management regarding Rh D isoimmunisation?

options.text: No anti-D needed as there is no vaginal bleeding
options.rationale: Abdominal trauma is a sensitising event regardless of whether vaginal bleeding is present. Fetomaternal haemorrhage can occur from placental disruption without external bleeding. Anti-D should be administered to all unsensitised Rh D-negative women after abdominal trauma. (Source: ANZSBT Anti-D Guidelines; RANZCOG Anti-D Guideline)

options.text: Administer anti-D 250 IU IM within 72 hours only
options.rationale: At 14 weeks gestation, the standard prophylactic dose of anti-D (250 IU in Australia) is given, but a Kleihauer-Betke test is not required before 20 weeks as the foetal blood volume is too small for significant fetomaternal haemorrhage quantification. However, the dose is correct for first-trimester/early-second-trimester events. The option is incomplete - anti-D should be given as soon as possible, ideally within 72 hours. (Source: ANZSBT Anti-D Guidelines)

options.text: Check Kleihauer-Betke test then give anti-D if positive
options.rationale: Before 20 weeks gestation, the Kleihauer-Betke test is not routinely performed as the foetal red cell volume is insufficient for reliable quantification. Anti-D is given empirically at the standard dose without waiting for Kleihauer results. After 20 weeks, Kleihauer is performed to quantify FMH and determine if additional anti-D is needed. (Source: ANZSBT Anti-D Guidelines; RANZCOG Anti-D Guideline)

options.text: Repeat indirect Coombs test and defer anti-D if negative
options.rationale: Repeating the indirect Coombs test does not replace the need for anti-D prophylaxis. The Coombs test detects existing antibodies, but anti-D is given to PREVENT sensitisation after the potentially sensitising event. A negative Coombs confirms she is not yet sensitised, which is the indication for prophylaxis. (Source: ANZSBT Anti-D Guidelines)

options.text: Administer anti-D 250 IU IM as soon as possible
options.rationale: Anti-D immunoglobulin 250 IU IM should be given as soon as possible after a potentially sensitising event in an unsensitised Rh D-negative woman, ideally within 72 hours. At less than 20 weeks gestation, the standard dose of 250 IU is sufficient without Kleihauer testing. Abdominal trauma is a recognised sensitising event. (Source: ANZSBT Anti-D Guidelines; RANZCOG Anti-D Guideline)

explanation.summary: Rh D-negative women should receive anti-D immunoglobulin after any potentially sensitising event to prevent isoimmunisation. Sensitising events include vaginal bleeding, abdominal trauma, invasive procedures, miscarriage, and ectopic pregnancy. Before 20 weeks, the standard dose of 250 IU is given without Kleihauer testing. After 20 weeks, 625 IU is the standard dose with Kleihauer performed to determine if additional doses are needed for large fetomaternal haemorrhage.
key_points: Anti-D given after any sensitising event in unsensitised Rh D-negative women
Before 20 weeks: 250 IU without Kleihauer
After 20 weeks: 625 IU with Kleihauer to quantify FMH
Anti-D should be given within 72 hours of the event
Routine antenatal prophylaxis at 28 and 34 weeks is also standard
explanation.pearls: The dose of anti-D in Australia changes at the 20-week threshold: 250 IU before 20 weeks (without Kleihauer) versus 625 IU from 20 weeks onwards (with Kleihauer to detect FMH >6 mL requiring additional doses).

subtopic_detail: Obstetric cholestasis bile acid threshold and delivery
stem: A 34-year-old woman at 35 weeks gestation presents with a 1-week history of generalised pruritus that is worse at night, particularly affecting her palms and soles. She has no rash. Liver function tests show mildly elevated transaminases. Her total serum bile acids return at 42 micromol/L. The foetal cardiotocograph is reassuring. She has had no complications during the pregnancy to date.

data_table.Investigations: ALT 68 U/L, AST 54 U/L, GGT 32 U/L, Bilirubin 14 micromol/L, Bile acids 42 micromol/L, Platelets 228 x10^9/L, CTG reassuring

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Commence UDCA and plan delivery at 39 weeks
options.rationale: Bile acids of 42 micromol/L represent moderate intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy (ICP). While UDCA is appropriate for symptom relief, a delivery plan at 39 weeks is only suitable for mild ICP (bile acids 19-39 micromol/L). At 40 micromol/L or above, earlier delivery is recommended due to increased stillbirth risk. (Source: RCOG Green-top Guideline: ICP; Queensland Clinical Guideline: ICP)

options.text: Reassure and repeat bile acids in one week
options.rationale: Bile acids of 42 micromol/L confirm the diagnosis of ICP and require active management. Delaying treatment and monitoring risks ongoing foetal exposure to elevated bile acids, which are associated with adverse foetal outcomes including preterm birth, meconium-stained liquor, and stillbirth. (Source: RCOG Green-top Guideline: ICP)

options.text: Commence UDCA and plan delivery at 37 weeks
options.rationale: With bile acids of 42 micromol/L (40-99 range), ursodeoxycholic acid should be commenced for symptom relief, and planned delivery at 37 weeks gestation is recommended. The stillbirth risk increases significantly when bile acids exceed 40 micromol/L, particularly above 100 micromol/L. Weekly bile acid and LFT monitoring is also indicated. (Source: RCOG Green-top Guideline: ICP; Queensland Clinical Guideline: ICP)

options.text: Immediate delivery via induction of labour now
options.rationale: Immediate delivery at 35 weeks for bile acids of 42 micromol/L exposes the neonate to unnecessary prematurity risks. Delivery at 37 weeks balances foetal maturity with stillbirth risk. Immediate delivery is reserved for bile acids above 100 micromol/L or if foetal compromise is demonstrated. (Source: RCOG Green-top Guideline: ICP)

options.text: Commence cholestyramine and monitor bile acids
options.rationale: Cholestyramine is a bile acid sequestrant that is poorly tolerated and less effective than UDCA for ICP symptoms. It also interferes with fat-soluble vitamin absorption, including vitamin K, which could worsen coagulopathy risk. UDCA is the preferred pharmacological treatment. (Source: AMH; RCOG Green-top Guideline: ICP)

explanation.summary: Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy is stratified by bile acid levels: mild (19-39 micromol/L, delivery at 39-40 weeks), moderate (40-99 micromol/L, delivery at 37 weeks), and severe (100+ micromol/L, consider immediate delivery or at 34-36 weeks with corticosteroids). UDCA is the mainstay of symptom treatment. Weekly bile acid and LFT monitoring is important as levels can fluctuate. Vitamin K supplementation may be needed if coagulation is affected.
key_points: Bile acids 40-99 micromol/L: moderate ICP, plan delivery at 37 weeks
Bile acids >100 micromol/L: severe ICP with highest stillbirth risk
UDCA is first-line pharmacological treatment for symptom relief
Weekly bile acid and LFT monitoring is recommended
explanation.pearls: Stillbirth risk in ICP rises sharply at bile acids above 100 micromol/L (prevalence 3.4%) compared to 40-99 micromol/L (0.3%) - this threshold drives the decision between 37-week delivery and earlier intervention.

subtopic_detail: Thyroid in pregnancy - levothyroxine TSH target
stem: A 30-year-old woman with pre-existing hypothyroidism attends at 8 weeks gestation. She takes levothyroxine 100 mcg daily, which has been stable for 2 years. She feels well with no symptoms of hypothyroidism. Her pre-pregnancy TSH was 1.8 mIU/L. Today's bloods show a TSH of 4.8 mIU/L with a free T4 of 12.2 pmol/L (reference 12-22).

data_table.Investigations: TSH 4.8 mIU/L, Free T4 12.2 pmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate adjustment to her levothyroxine dose?

options.text: Increase levothyroxine to 125 mcg daily promptly
options.rationale: In pregnancy, the target TSH is below 2.5 mIU/L in the first trimester (and below 3.0 in later trimesters per some guidelines). Her TSH of 4.8 exceeds this target. Levothyroxine requirements typically increase by 30-50% in pregnancy. An empirical increase of 25-30% (100 to 125 mcg) is appropriate with recheck in 4-6 weeks. (Source: ATA Thyroid in Pregnancy Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

options.text: Maintain current dose and recheck TSH in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Maintaining the current dose is inappropriate as TSH of 4.8 mIU/L in early pregnancy is above the recommended threshold. Untreated maternal hypothyroidism in the first trimester is associated with impaired foetal neurodevelopment. Prompt dose adjustment is needed. (Source: ATA Thyroid in Pregnancy Guidelines)

options.text: Increase levothyroxine to 200 mcg daily immediately
options.rationale: Doubling the dose from 100 to 200 mcg is excessive and risks iatrogenic hyperthyroidism. A dose increase of 25-50% is recommended. The TSH of 4.8 requires correction but not a dramatic dose change. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology; AMH)

options.text: Add liothyronine 10 mcg daily to current dose
options.rationale: Combination T4/T3 therapy is not recommended in pregnancy. Liothyronine does not cross the placenta as effectively as T4, and the evidence base supports levothyroxine monotherapy for maternal hypothyroidism in pregnancy. (Source: ATA Thyroid in Pregnancy Guidelines)

options.text: Switch to desiccated thyroid extract preparation
options.rationale: Desiccated thyroid extract contains variable T3/T4 ratios and is not recommended in pregnancy due to inconsistent dosing. Levothyroxine monotherapy is the standard of care. The correct approach is to increase the levothyroxine dose. (Source: ATA Thyroid in Pregnancy Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Levothyroxine requirements increase by 30-50% during pregnancy, primarily due to increased thyroxine-binding globulin, expanded plasma volume, and placental deiodination. The TSH target in pregnancy is below 2.5 mIU/L in the first trimester. Dose adjustments should be made promptly when TSH rises above target, with TSH rechecked every 4-6 weeks. A practical strategy is to increase the weekly dose by 2 extra tablets immediately upon pregnancy confirmation in women on established replacement.
key_points: TSH target <2.5 mIU/L in first trimester of pregnancy
Levothyroxine requirements increase 30-50% in pregnancy
Dose adjustment should be prompt when TSH is above target
Recheck TSH every 4-6 weeks during pregnancy until stable
explanation.pearls: A practical approach to thyroid replacement in early pregnancy is the '2 extra tablets per week' rule - women on stable levothyroxine should take 2 additional tablets per week (approximately 29% increase) upon pregnancy confirmation while awaiting formal TSH testing.

stem: A 28-year-old woman at 32 weeks gestation is found to have a haemoglobin of 88 g/L at routine blood testing. She reports fatigue and breathlessness on exertion. Her ferritin is 6 microg/L. She has been taking oral iron (ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily) for 4 weeks prescribed at her 28-week visit when her Hb was 102 g/L, but reports significant nausea and constipation limiting adherence. She has no bleeding.

data_table.Investigations: Hb 88 g/L, MCV 74 fL, Ferritin 6 microg/L, CRP 4 mg/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Switch to oral iron polymaltose every second day
options.rationale: While alternate-day dosing and switching formulations may improve tolerance, this patient's Hb has dropped from 102 to 88 g/L despite 4 weeks of oral iron. At 32 weeks with depleted ferritin (6 microg/L) and worsening anaemia, there is insufficient time for oral iron to replenish stores before delivery. IV iron is indicated. (Source: ANZSBT Iron Deficiency Anaemia Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines)

options.text: Increase oral iron to twice daily with vitamin C
options.rationale: Increasing the dose will likely worsen her GI side effects, which are already limiting adherence. Additionally, the declining Hb despite oral supplementation and severely depleted ferritin at 32 weeks indicates that oral iron alone is unlikely to correct the anaemia before delivery. (Source: ANZSBT Iron Deficiency Anaemia Guidelines)

options.text: Arrange packed red blood cell transfusion now
options.rationale: Blood transfusion is reserved for Hb below 70 g/L in stable patients or when there is haemodynamic compromise. This patient's Hb of 88 g/L with stable vital signs does not meet transfusion criteria. IV iron is the appropriate intervention. (Source: NBA Patient Blood Management Guidelines)

options.text: Recheck iron studies in 2 weeks before deciding
options.rationale: Delaying further investigation and treatment when there is progressive iron deficiency anaemia at 32 weeks is inappropriate. The diagnosis is clear (ferritin 6 microg/L, microcytic anaemia failing oral therapy), and delivery may be only 5-8 weeks away. IV iron should be commenced promptly. (Source: ANZSBT Iron Deficiency Anaemia Guidelines)

options.text: Commence IV iron infusion (ferric carboxymaltose)
options.rationale: IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose or iron polymaltose) is indicated when oral iron is not tolerated, fails to correct anaemia, or when rapid correction is needed in the third trimester. This patient meets all three criteria: intolerance, declining Hb, and limited time before delivery. A single dose of ferric carboxymaltose (up to 1000 mg) can be given after the first trimester. (Source: ANZSBT Iron Deficiency Anaemia Guidelines; RANZCOG; AMH)

explanation.summary: Iron deficiency anaemia in pregnancy requires prompt correction as it increases risks of preterm birth, low birth weight, and peripartum transfusion. IV iron is indicated when oral iron is not tolerated, has failed, or when rapid correction is needed in the third trimester. Ferric carboxymaltose allows a single infusion of up to 1000 mg and can be given from the second trimester onwards. Hb response is expected within 2-3 weeks. Transfusion is reserved for severe anaemia (Hb <70) or haemodynamic instability.
key_points: IV iron indicated when oral iron fails, is not tolerated, or rapid correction needed
Ferric carboxymaltose up to 1000 mg can be given as a single infusion
IV iron is safe from the second trimester onwards
Hb response expected within 2-3 weeks of IV iron
explanation.pearls: Ferric carboxymaltose raises haemoglobin by approximately 20 g/L within 2-3 weeks of a single 1000 mg infusion - making it the preferred parenteral iron preparation in late pregnancy when time to delivery is limited.

subtopic_detail: VTE prophylaxis risk assessment in pregnancy
stem: A 35-year-old woman presents at 10 weeks gestation for her booking visit. She had a provoked deep vein thrombosis 3 years ago following a long-haul flight, treated with rivaroxaban for 3 months. She has no family history of VTE. Thrombophilia screening performed after the DVT was negative. Her BMI is 28. She is otherwise well with no other risk factors.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate thromboprophylaxis plan for this pregnancy?

options.text: No pharmacological prophylaxis is required in this pregnancy
options.rationale: A previous VTE, even if provoked, confers increased risk during pregnancy. The hypercoagulable state of pregnancy represents a significant prothrombotic challenge. Pharmacological prophylaxis is warranted. (Source: RCOG Green-top Guideline 37a: VTE in Pregnancy; RANZCOG VTE Guideline)

options.text: Prophylactic LMWH from 28 weeks until 6 weeks postpartum
options.rationale: For a single previous provoked VTE with no thrombophilia and no other major risk factors, RCOG and RANZCOG recommend antenatal LMWH prophylaxis from 28 weeks gestation continuing for 6 weeks postpartum. Earlier commencement (from first trimester) is reserved for higher-risk women such as those with unprovoked VTE, thrombophilia, or multiple VTEs. (Source: RCOG Green-top Guideline 37a; RANZCOG VTE Guideline)

options.text: Continue rivaroxaban at current dose throughout pregnancy
options.rationale: Direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) including rivaroxaban are contraindicated in pregnancy due to teratogenicity and placental transfer. Low molecular weight heparin is the anticoagulant of choice in pregnancy as it does not cross the placenta. (Source: AMH; RANZCOG VTE Guideline)

options.text: Prophylactic LMWH from now until 6 weeks after delivery
options.rationale: First-trimester LMWH prophylaxis is indicated for higher-risk women: unprovoked VTE, known thrombophilia with previous VTE, recurrent VTE, or antiphospholipid syndrome. This woman had a single provoked event with negative thrombophilia screen, placing her in the intermediate-risk category suitable for prophylaxis from 28 weeks. (Source: RCOG Green-top Guideline 37a)

options.text: Prophylactic LMWH for the 6 weeks postpartum period only
options.rationale: Postnatal-only prophylaxis is used for women with risk factors but no personal history of VTE. A prior DVT, even if provoked, warrants antenatal prophylaxis in the third trimester in addition to postnatal prophylaxis. (Source: RCOG Green-top Guideline 37a; RANZCOG VTE Guideline)

explanation.summary: VTE risk in pregnancy is 5-10 times higher than in non-pregnant women. Risk stratification guides prophylaxis timing. Women with a single prior provoked VTE and no thrombophilia typically commence LMWH from 28 weeks until 6 weeks postpartum. Higher-risk women (unprovoked VTE, thrombophilia, recurrent events) start from the first trimester. DOACs and warfarin are avoided in pregnancy. LMWH is safe as it does not cross the placenta.
key_points: Single provoked VTE with no thrombophilia: LMWH from 28 weeks to 6 weeks postpartum
Unprovoked VTE or thrombophilia: LMWH from first trimester
DOACs are contraindicated in pregnancy
LMWH does not cross the placenta and is safe for the foetus
explanation.pearls: The thrombotic risk stratification in pregnancy hinges on whether a prior VTE was provoked versus unprovoked: provoked events with negative thrombophilia screen carry approximately 2-3% recurrence risk in pregnancy, while unprovoked events carry 6-10% recurrence risk.

stem: You are the registrar attending a vaginal delivery. After delivery of the head, the chin retracts tightly against the perineum - the turtle sign. Gentle downward traction on the head does not deliver the anterior shoulder. The time is noted and the emergency buzzer is pressed. The mother is in lithotomy position. An assistant has arrived.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate manoeuvre?

options.text: Apply fundal pressure from above the uterus
options.rationale: Fundal pressure is contraindicated in shoulder dystocia. It pushes the anterior shoulder further into the symphysis pubis, worsening impaction and increasing the risk of brachial plexus injury. It should never be applied. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia Guideline; ALSO Course Manual)

options.text: Perform Zavanelli manoeuvre to replace the head
options.rationale: The Zavanelli manoeuvre (cephalic replacement followed by caesarean) is a last-resort intervention used only when all other manoeuvres have failed. It should not be attempted as a first-line response. First-line manoeuvres (McRoberts, suprapubic pressure) resolve the majority of shoulder dystocias. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia Guideline)

options.text: Perform an episiotomy to create more space
options.rationale: Episiotomy alone does not relieve bony shoulder impaction, as shoulder dystocia is a bony obstruction at the pelvic inlet. However, episiotomy may facilitate internal manoeuvres if needed subsequently. It is not the immediate first-line action. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia Guideline)

options.text: Apply McRoberts positioning with suprapubic pressure
options.rationale: McRoberts manoeuvre (hyperflexion of the maternal hips onto the abdomen) combined with suprapubic pressure (applied by an assistant above the symphysis to dislodge the anterior shoulder) is the first-line intervention for shoulder dystocia. It resolves 40-60% of cases by straightening the lumbosacral angle and rotating the pelvis. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia Guideline; ALSO Course Manual)

options.text: Attempt rotational delivery of the posterior arm
options.rationale: Delivery of the posterior arm (Barnum manoeuvre) or rotational manoeuvres (Rubin II, Wood's screw) are effective but are second-line interventions used when McRoberts and suprapubic pressure fail. The HELPERR mnemonic dictates the sequence, with McRoberts and suprapubic pressure first. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia Guideline; ALSO Course Manual)

explanation.summary: Shoulder dystocia occurs when the foetal shoulders fail to deliver after the head, typically due to impaction of the anterior shoulder behind the symphysis pubis. The HELPERR mnemonic guides management: Help, Evaluate for episiotomy, Legs (McRoberts), suprapubic Pressure, Enter (internal rotation/posterior arm), Roll (all-fours), Replace (Zavanelli). McRoberts with suprapubic pressure resolves 40-60% of cases and should always be performed first. Fundal pressure is absolutely contraindicated.
key_points: McRoberts positioning with suprapubic pressure is the first-line manoeuvre
Fundal pressure is contraindicated and worsens impaction
Internal rotation and posterior arm delivery are second-line
Document the time from head to body delivery and all manoeuvres used
explanation.pearls: Suprapubic pressure should be applied in a downward and lateral direction (CPR-style, not rocking) above the symphysis pubis to push the anterior shoulder away from the midline - this is distinct from fundal pressure which is never applied in shoulder dystocia.

stem: You are the obstetric registrar called urgently to the birth suite. A 26-year-old woman at 37 weeks gestation had spontaneous rupture of membranes 5 minutes ago. The midwife felt the umbilical cord prolapsing through the cervix during a vaginal examination. The cervix is 5 cm dilated. The foetal heart rate on CTG shows a prolonged deceleration to 80 bpm. The operating theatre is available.

data_table.Investigations: CTG: foetal bradycardia 80 bpm, cervix 5 cm dilated, cord palpable below presenting part

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate action while preparing for delivery?

options.text: Clamp the cord and prepare for vaginal delivery
options.rationale: Clamping the prolapsed cord would completely occlude blood flow to the foetus and cause rapid foetal demise. The cord must be kept warm and moist, and compression must be relieved. The cervix is only 5 cm dilated, so vaginal delivery is not imminent. (Source: RANZCOG Cord Prolapse Guideline; Queensland Clinical Guideline: Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Commence oxytocin to expedite vaginal delivery
options.rationale: Augmenting labour with oxytocin is inappropriate. At 5 cm dilatation with a prolapsed cord, vaginal delivery cannot be achieved rapidly enough. Category 1 emergency caesarean section is the appropriate mode of delivery. Oxytocin would increase uterine contractions, worsening cord compression. (Source: RANZCOG Cord Prolapse Guideline)

options.text: Elevate the presenting part digitally per vaginam
options.rationale: The most critical immediate action is continuous digital elevation of the presenting part off the prolapsed cord to relieve compression. This should be maintained until emergency caesarean section is performed. The patient should be placed in the left lateral or knee-chest position to reduce cord compression, and the cord kept warm with moist gauze. (Source: RANZCOG Cord Prolapse Guideline; Queensland Clinical Guideline: Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Administer terbutaline 250 mcg SC for tocolysis
options.rationale: Tocolysis may be considered as an adjunct to reduce uterine contractions and cord compression, but it is not the most important immediate action. Digital elevation of the presenting part is the priority to relieve cord compression. Tocolysis may be given concurrently but should not delay manual decompression. (Source: Queensland Clinical Guideline: Cord Prolapse)

options.text: Attempt to manually replace the cord above the cervix
options.rationale: Attempting to replace the cord (funic reduction) is associated with cord spasm and further compression. It is not recommended as standard practice. Digital elevation of the presenting part is the proven emergency manoeuvre while arranging caesarean delivery. (Source: RANZCOG Cord Prolapse Guideline)

explanation.summary: Cord prolapse is an obstetric emergency requiring immediate action to prevent foetal hypoxia and death. The key interventions are: digital elevation of the presenting part to relieve cord compression, maternal repositioning (knee-chest or left lateral with head down), keeping the cord warm and moist, and emergency category 1 caesarean section. Bladder filling with 500-700 mL normal saline via a Foley catheter can also elevate the presenting part while transferring to theatre. Tocolysis may reduce uterine contractions as an adjunct.
key_points: Digital elevation of the presenting part is the critical immediate action
Do not attempt to replace the cord above the cervix
Emergency category 1 caesarean section is the definitive management
Bladder filling can be used as an adjunct to elevate the presenting part
explanation.pearls: Rapid bladder filling with 500-700 mL normal saline via Foley catheter provides sustained elevation of the presenting part off the prolapsed cord - this 'hands-free' technique frees the operator during transfer to theatre and is taught in the ALSO and PROMPT courses.

stem: A 33-year-old woman at 30 weeks gestation was diagnosed with GDM at 26 weeks. She has been on dietary modification and walking 30 minutes daily for 4 weeks. Her BSL diary over the past week shows consistent fasting readings of 5.3-5.6 mmol/L and 2-hour postprandial readings of 6.8-7.2 mmol/L. She has gained 1.5 kg in 4 weeks. The foetal growth scan shows an estimated foetal weight on the 72nd centile with a normal amniotic fluid index.

data_table.Investigations: BSL diary: fasting 5.3-5.6 mmol/L, 2h postprandial 6.8-7.2 mmol/L, EFW 72nd centile, AFI normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in her glycaemic management?

options.text: Continue dietary management and review in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Her fasting BSL consistently exceeds the ADIPS target of less than 5.0 mmol/L, despite 4 weeks of dietary modification. Continuing the same approach when targets are not being met delays necessary pharmacological intervention and risks macrosomia and other GDM complications. (Source: ADIPS GDM Guidelines)

options.text: Add metformin 500 mg with dinner and titrate
options.rationale: Metformin is a reasonable first-line pharmacological agent for GDM, particularly for postprandial hyperglycaemia and insulin resistance. However, when fasting hyperglycaemia is the primary problem (as here, with fasting readings 5.3-5.6 consistently above target), insulin is more effective at controlling overnight hepatic glucose output. (Source: ADIPS GDM Guidelines; AMH)

options.text: Refer to endocrinology before starting any medication
options.rationale: Initiation of insulin for GDM is within the scope of obstetric and diabetes-in-pregnancy teams and does not require endocrinology referral. Delaying treatment for a specialist referral when targets are clearly not being met is inappropriate at 30 weeks. (Source: ADIPS GDM Guidelines)

options.text: Commence glibenclamide 2.5 mg with breakfast
options.rationale: Glibenclamide (glyburide) crosses the placenta and is associated with neonatal hypoglycaemia. It is not recommended as first-line in Australian GDM guidelines. When fasting BSL is persistently elevated, insulin provides more predictable and titratable control. (Source: ADIPS GDM Guidelines; AMH)

options.text: Commence evening intermediate-acting insulin (NPH)
options.rationale: Intermediate-acting insulin (NPH or isophane) given at bedtime is the most appropriate treatment for persistent fasting hyperglycaemia in GDM that has not responded to lifestyle modification. It controls overnight hepatic glucose production. The ADIPS fasting target is less than 5.0 mmol/L. Starting dose is typically 4-8 units, titrated based on fasting BSL readings. (Source: ADIPS GDM Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines)

explanation.summary: GDM management follows a stepwise approach: lifestyle modification first, then pharmacological therapy if targets are not met within 1-2 weeks. The ADIPS targets are fasting less than 5.0 mmol/L and 2-hour postprandial less than 6.7 mmol/L. Persistent fasting hyperglycaemia is best addressed with evening/bedtime intermediate-acting insulin (NPH), which controls overnight hepatic glucose output. Metformin is more useful for postprandial hyperglycaemia and insulin resistance.
key_points: ADIPS fasting BSL target is <5.0 mmol/L in GDM
Persistent fasting hyperglycaemia indicates need for bedtime NPH insulin
Metformin is preferred when postprandial readings are the main issue
Lifestyle modification should be trialled for 1-2 weeks before pharmacotherapy
explanation.pearls: The choice between metformin and insulin in GDM depends on the glycaemic pattern: fasting hyperglycaemia responds better to bedtime NPH insulin (targeting overnight hepatic glucose output), while isolated postprandial hyperglycaemia may respond to metformin alone.

subtopic_detail: HELLP syndrome vs severe pre-eclampsia differentiation
stem: A 32-year-old primigravida at 33 weeks gestation presents with epigastric pain and nausea for 12 hours. Her blood pressure is 156/102 mmHg. She reports visual disturbances. Her bloods show platelets 68 x10^9/L, ALT 342 U/L, AST 298 U/L, LDH 840 U/L, and a blood film showing schistocytes. Her Hb is 98 g/L (was 124 at 28 weeks). Urine protein:creatinine ratio is 85 mg/mmol. She received a loading dose of magnesium sulfate and IV labetalol.

data_table.Investigations: Plt 68 x10^9/L, ALT 342, AST 298, LDH 840, Hb 98, blood film: schistocytes, uPCR 85 mg/mmol, Cr 78 micromol/L, fibrinogen 3.2 g/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate definitive management plan?

options.text: Administer betamethasone and deliver at 34 weeks
options.rationale: While corticosteroids for foetal lung maturation are appropriate, delaying delivery for a full 48 hours until 34 weeks is not safe in this clinical situation. HELLP syndrome with thrombocytopaenia below 100, significantly elevated transaminases, and haemolysis is an indication for delivery once initial maternal stabilisation is achieved, regardless of gestation. (Source: ISSHP Classification 2018; SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)

options.text: Commence plasma exchange for microangiopathic haemolysis
options.rationale: Plasma exchange is the treatment for thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP), not HELLP syndrome. While both cause microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia and thrombocytopaenia, HELLP is characterised by elevated transaminases (typically mild in TTP) and resolves with delivery. ADAMTS13 activity testing can differentiate if clinical doubt exists. (Source: ISSHP Classification 2018)

options.text: Give betamethasone now and deliver within 24-48 hours
options.rationale: HELLP syndrome (Haemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets) in this presentation requires delivery as definitive treatment. Betamethasone should be administered for foetal lung maturation, and delivery planned within 24-48 hours to allow maximum steroid benefit while limiting maternal risk. The timing reflects the severity - platelets below 100 with active haemolysis and markedly elevated transaminases do not permit prolonged expectant management. (Source: ISSHP Classification 2018; SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy; RANZCOG Pre-eclampsia Guideline)

options.text: Manage expectantly with daily bloods until 37 weeks
options.rationale: Expectant management to 37 weeks is not appropriate with HELLP syndrome. The condition carries risks of hepatic rupture, DIC, placental abruption, and maternal death. HELLP with platelets below 100 and markedly elevated transaminases mandates delivery. Expectant management is only considered in mild pre-eclampsia without end-organ damage before 37 weeks. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy)
